source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5527__American-Public-Power-Association/20251121 - RM26-4 - APPA Comments on Large Load Interconnection ANOPR.txt
overall_summary: APPA supports the ANOPR's respect for state and local jurisdiction over retail transactions and proposes that any interconnection rules must allow states to regulate retail rates and terms. APPA opposes mandating retail wheeling and seeks protections for existing customers from stranded costs through well-tailored deposit and commitment requirements.

quotes.text: APPA applauds the Commission for its extensive efforts in recent years to address issues associated with large data center loads and for its careful analysis and respect for the Federal Power Act's clear jurisdictional lines that reserve authority for retail transactions to state and local regulatory authorities.
quotes.concern: Support for FERC respect of state/local retail jurisdiction

quotes.text: APPA urges the Commission to consider how to implement and modify where necessary the ANOPR's principles in a way that achieves two goals: (1) continuing to allow relevant retail regulatory authorities to oversee the rates, terms, and conditions of retail service; and (2) ensuring that existing customers do not bear increased cost or increased risk as a result of Commission-jurisdictional interconnection rules for large loads.
quotes.concern: Core request for state retail authority preservation and existing customer protection

quotes.text: Any final Commission action should explicitly allow relevant retail regulatory authorities to continue regulating the rates, terms, and conditions of retail service, including allocation of costs for generation built to serve new large loads.
quotes.concern: State regulatory authority must retain control over retail rates and generation cost allocation

quotes.text: The Federal Power Act limits the Commission's jurisdiction to transmission of electric energy in interstate commerce and the sales of such energy at wholesale in interstate commerce. That jurisdiction extends only to those matters which are not subject to regulation by the States.
quotes.concern: Legal basis that FERC jurisdiction is limited to wholesale transactions

quotes.text: Thus retail sales and all associated terms and conditions including the assessment of generation costs and establishment of minimum required contractual provisions are reserved to state commissions.
quotes.concern: Retail cost assessment and contract terms are state authority

quotes.text: Any final Commission action should not explicitly or implicitly mandate retail wheeling.
In Order No. 888, the Commission prudently declined to exert authority over the transmission component of bundled retail sales, while reserving the issue of whether it had jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: Historical precedent for not regulating bundled transmission

quotes.text: Whatever final action the Commission takes on the ANOPR, it should limit the scope of that action to exclude transmission that is bundled with a retail sale.
quotes.concern: Bundled transmission should remain excluded from FERC interconnection rules

quotes.text: New large loads must pay their fair share of infrastructure costs that are needed to serve them.
quotes.concern: Large loads must cover their proportional infrastructure costs

quotes.text: Wholesale transmission customers in particular could be left shouldering massive costs if new large loads do not ultimately attain or maintain the level of electricity demand that is being forecasted. The risk of stranded costs must therefore be allocated to the entities that are causing the new investment.
quotes.concern: Existing wholesale customers at risk of stranded costs from demand shortfall

quotes.text: The ANOPR's proposal to impose standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties appears to be consistent with the objective of fairly allocating costs.
quotes.concern: Support for ANOPR cost allocation mechanisms

quotes.text: But such commitments must be well-tailored to the specific risks posed by large loads and hybrid interconnections, and they must account for regional variations and variations in interconnection configurations.
quotes.concern: Cost commitment requirements should reflect hybrid risks and regional differences

quotes.text: The ANOPR's proposal to allocate all network upgrade costs to newly interconnecting large loads would insulate existing customers from costs that would not be caused but for the new interconnection.
quotes.concern: Network upgrade cost allocation to new loads protects existing customers

quotes.text: But such a principle should not disrupt the existing, long-standing cost allocation mechanisms that exist for planning the overall transmission system to meet the needs of load serving entities.
quotes.concern: New cost allocation must not disrupt existing system planning mechanisms

bins.name: State retail jurisdiction preservation
bins.description: APPA strongly supports explicit Commission recognition that state and local regulators must retain authority over retail rates, terms, and generation cost allocation for large loads. Federal Power Act limits FERC to wholesale; retail and bundled transmission must remain state-regulated.

bins.name: Hybrid interconnection cost tailoring
bins.description: Cost commitment mechanisms and deposit requirements should account for specific risks of hybrid interconnections and regional variations in transmission configurations.

bins.name: Existing customer cost protection
bins.description: New large loads must pay their fair share of infrastructure costs. Existing customers and wholesale transmission customers must be insulated from stranded costs if demand forecasts are not met. Network upgrade costs should be allocated to new loads. Crediting mechanisms must not harm other transmission customers.

bins.name: Study deposits and cost fairness
bins.description: ANOPR's standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties are consistent with fair cost allocation. These commitments must be well-tailored to large load and hybrid interconnection risks.

bins.name: Opposition to retail wheeling mandate
bins.description: APPA opposes any Commission rule that explicitly or implicitly mandates retail wheeling. Federal Power Act prohibits such orders.

bins.name: Bundled transmission exemption
bins.description: Commission should preserve its longstanding Order 888 approach of excluding bundled transmission from FERC regulation. Final ANOPR action should not extend to transmission bundled with retail sales.
